coaching.coaching: Realign analyze signature alignment with parent epic interface to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.
coaching.subject: analyze signature alignment with parent epic interface

coaching.coaching: Plan how backfill invocation verification command adequacy will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: backfill invocation verification command adequacy

coaching.coaching: Plan how percentage field propagation through AnalyzerResult will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: percentage field propagation through AnalyzerResult

coaching.coaching: Pin down run_jscpd return type and signature contract with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: run_jscpd return type and signature contract

coaching.coaching: Plan how malformed or incomplete jscpd JSON output handling will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: malformed or incomplete jscpd JSON output handling

coaching.coaching: Pin down non-zero exit code failure path specification with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: non-zero exit code failure path specification

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] statistics.total.percentage key existence in jscpd output will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: statistics.total.percentage key existence in jscpd output

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the adapter's public signature to `analyze(repo_root, languages)` matching the parent epic's interface definition, and update AC3's proving command accordingly (e.g. `analyze(root, languages=["JavaScript"])`).
evidence: Epic 839b: "Define an analyzer interface: `analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}`"
Leaf AC3: "`analyze(root)` on a known-clone fixture returns `.duplication.clones >= 1`" — single positional arg, languages omitted
3b30 (interface blocker) defines the Analyzer protocol and AnalyzerResult but does not re-state the method signature — the epic's two-arg definition is the authoritative contract
findings.finding: The leaf's AC3 tests `analyze(root)` (single-argument), but the parent epic (839b) defines the interface as `analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}`. The leaf's adapter omits the `languages` parameter, contradicting the parent's named interface contract.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria AC3: 'analyze(root) on a known-clone fixture returns .duplication.clones >= 1'; Epic 839b Design section: 'analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
scenarios: If other analyzer adapters (scc, lizard) implement `analyze(repo_root, languages)` per the epic's spec, the jscpd adapter will be interface-incompatible, breaking the `resolve_analyzer` dispatch layer that expects a uniform protocol.
Test `test_live_clone` as written will pass a single-arg call, masking the missing `languages` parameter until integration.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the adapter method to `def analyze(self, repo_root: Path, languages: list[str]) -> AnalyzerResult` (or accept `languages` as an optional kwarg if jscpd is language-agnostic) and update AC3 to `analyze(root, ["JavaScript"])` to match the epic's two-argument interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC5's proof to assert the full argument list is preserved (e.g. a unit test asserting `_jscpd.run_jscpd` is called with the same args the old inline code used, or a snapshot of the subprocess call), and add an explicit rollback note (e.g. 'revert delegate if backfill regression detected').
evidence: Plan claims 'invocation byte-for-byte unchanged = still bare jscpd'
AC5 proves only: grep -q "\[.\?.jscpd" scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py
A grep for the binary name cannot verify argument list, flags, or output-path arguments are unchanged
findings.finding: AC5's proving command (`grep -q "\[.\?.jscpd" scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`) is too loose to verify the 'byte-for-byte unchanged' invocation claim — it only confirms the binary name appears, not that arguments, flags, or output paths are preserved after the extraction refactor. If the extraction introduces an argument regression, AC5 passes but behavior changes silently.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC5 / Approach & rationale
scenarios: Extraction changes the jscpd CLI flags (e.g. --reporter path) but grep still passes → backfill silently produces different output
CI passes; production backfill runs produce wrong statistics
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the loose grep with a test that captures the subprocess arguments passed by the backfill script and asserts they match the pre-refactor invocation exactly (e.g. mock `subprocess.run` and assert `call_args`). Add one sentence in the plan: 'Rollback: revert backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py to inline runner if regression detected before next scheduled backfill run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion to AC3 (or a dedicated AC) that `analyze(root).duplication.percentage` is a float > 0 on the known-clone fixture, covering the end-to-end flow of the percentage field through the adapter.
evidence: Description states AnalyzerResult.duplication contains 'clone count + duplicated-lines %'
AC1 verifies run_jscpd parses statistics.total.percentage (float)
AC3 asserts only .duplication.clones >= 1 — no assertion on .duplication.percentage
findings.finding: The `duplication.percentage` field (float) is verified only inside `run_jscpd` (AC1 unit-tests the parser) but no AC verifies it flows through the adapter's `AnalyzerResult.duplication` output — leaving the adapter's percentage surface uncovered.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC1 vs AC3; Description ('duplicated-lines %')
scenarios: Adapter maps only clones and drops percentage; AC1 and AC3 both pass; the described deliverable (duplicated-lines % in AnalyzerResult) is silently absent
findings.suggested_fix: Extend test_live_clone to assert `result.duplication.percentage > 0.0` alongside the existing `clones >= 1` check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `run_jscpd`'s concrete return type and signature (e.g. `-> tuple[int, float] | None` or a named dataclass with `clones` and `percentage` fields), and explicitly state how the backfill call-site is updated to consume the richer return value.
evidence: Backfill `_measure_clone_count` returns `int | None` (line 83: `return int(data['statistics']['total']['clones'])`)
Plan says 'Extract the backfill jscpd runner' implying a near-direct lift, but the adapter also needs `.percentage` which is not in the current seam
Plan never states `run_jscpd`'s return type, parameter list, or how the backfill call-site is updated to consume the new signature
ABSENCE: no return-type or signature spec for `run_jscpd` anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The shared `run_jscpd` helper's return type/signature is never specified in the plan. The existing backfill seam (`_measure_clone_count`) returns `int | None`; the new adapter needs both `clones` (int) and `percentage` (float). If `run_jscpd` returns a richer structure (e.g. a dataclass or tuple), the backfill call-site must be updated to destructure it — but the plan says the 'invocation [is] byte-for-byte unchanged', which is about the subprocess call, not the return shape. Without an explicit interface contract, the implementer must guess the return shape, risking a mismatch between the two call-sites that the 'single runner' guarantee cannot prevent.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / AC line 'run_jscpd in analyzers/_jscpd.py parses statistics.total.clones (int) + statistics.total.percentage (float)'
scenarios: Implementer makes `run_jscpd` return a dict/namedtuple with both fields; backfill call-site must be updated to extract `.clones` — but the plan implies the backfill is only 'delegated' with 'invocation unchanged'
Implementer makes `run_jscpd` return only `int` (matching current seam) and the adapter cannot obtain `percentage`
Two implementers working from this spec independently produce incompatible interfaces at the two call-sites
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: '`run_jscpd(root: str | Path) -> tuple[int, float] | None` — returns `(clones, percentage)` or `None` on non-zero returncode/missing report; raises `FileNotFoundError` on absent binary (caller converts to `Unavailable`). The backfill call-site is updated from `_measure_clone_count(tree)` to `run_jscpd(tree)[0]` (clone-int only).' Update AC1 proving command to assert the return type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test) for `run_jscpd` receiving malformed/partial JSON output (missing `statistics.total`, empty dict, non-zero exit code) — assert the adapter returns `Unavailable` (or a defined fallback) rather than raising KeyError/TypeError.
evidence: AC line 1 covers only the happy-path parse of `statistics.total.clones` + `statistics.total.percentage`.
AC line 4 covers only `FileNotFoundError` (binary absent); no AC covers jscpd exits non-zero, returns empty JSON, or returns JSON missing `statistics.total`.
ABSENCE: no test name or proving command for malformed/partial output handling is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The testing plan has no test for malformed or incomplete jscpd JSON output (e.g., missing `statistics.total`, null values, unexpected schema), leaving a silent failure mode unspecified: the new `run_jscpd` parser could raise a KeyError/TypeError that propagates to callers instead of returning `Unavailable`.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC line 1 (`test_parse_statistics_total`) and AC line 4 (`test_absent_jscpd_unavailable`)
scenarios: jscpd exits with a non-zero code on an unsupported language and writes partial JSON; parser raises KeyError and the backfill script crashes.
jscpd version mismatch changes the report schema; `statistics.total.percentage` is renamed; silent AttributeError propagates to callers.
Empty repository: jscpd writes `{}` or omits `statistics`; parser raises KeyError instead of returning a zero-clone result or Unavailable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `- [ ] jscpd returns malformed/partial JSON (monkeypatch runner to return `{}` or raise `json.JSONDecodeError`): `analyze(root)` returns `Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)`, not a KeyError. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_jscpd_dup.py::test_malformed_output_unavailable`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) covering the 'jscpd present but non-zero returncode' → `Unavailable` path, and include a corresponding unit test (`test_nonzero_returncode_unavailable`) to close the error-contract gap.
evidence: AC4 covers only 'jscpd ABSENT (monkeypatched runner raising FileNotFoundError)'
Backfill `_measure_clone_count` lines 77–78: `if proc.returncode != 0: return None` — a distinct failure mode from FileNotFoundError
ABSENCE: no AC or test for 'jscpd present, non-zero returncode → Unavailable'
findings.finding: The plan specifies only the FileNotFoundError → Unavailable failure path (AC4), but not the 'jscpd is present and reachable but returns a non-zero exit code' → Unavailable path. The existing backfill returns `None` for non-zero returncode (line 77–78), which the caller converts to a skip, but the new adapter must emit `Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)` — not `None`. The plan's AC coverage gap means the implementer may propagate `None` or raise rather than produce `Unavailable` for this failure mode.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC4 ('jscpd ABSENT … returns Unavailable')
scenarios: jscpd is installed but exits non-zero on an empty/trivial repo; adapter returns None or raises instead of Unavailable, breaking the consumer contract
jscpd JSON report file is absent (e.g. jscpd exited 0 but wrote no file); another unspecified edge case with undefined behavior

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in AC or Approach) that `run_jscpd` catches non-zero exit codes, JSON parse errors, and missing `statistics.total` keys and returns/raises in a documented, uniform way — ideally mapping all to `Unavailable` — and add a corresponding test (e.g. `test_jscpd_crash_unavailable`).
evidence: AC4 covers only FileNotFoundError monkeypatched path
No AC or prose addresses CalledProcessError, JSONDecodeError, or KeyError on statistics.total
ABSENCE: no mention of try/except around JSON parse or subprocess.run check=True/returncode handling
findings.finding: The plan specifies graceful degradation only for `FileNotFoundError` (jscpd absent); it does not specify how `run_jscpd` handles other subprocess failure modes — non-zero exit code, malformed/missing JSON, missing `statistics.total` keys — leaving the adapter potentially raising uncaught exceptions instead of returning `Unavailable`.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC4 ('jscpd ABSENT … FileNotFoundError'); Approach & rationale; analyzers/_jscpd.py
scenarios: jscpd is installed but crashes on a particular repo (non-zero exit) → unhandled CalledProcessError propagates to caller
jscpd outputs partial/malformed JSON (e.g. disk full) → JSONDecodeError propagates
Future jscpd version changes output schema, statistics.total absent → KeyError propagates
findings.suggested_fix: In `_jscpd.run_jscpd`, wrap the subprocess call and JSON parse in a broad try/except catching `FileNotFoundError`, `subprocess.CalledProcessError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`, and `KeyError`, and return `Unavailable(reason=str(e), accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE)` for all. Add an AC: 'jscpd exits non-zero or outputs invalid JSON: returns Unavailable, not a raise. PROVE: test_jscpd_crash_unavailable'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that jscpd's JSON report actually emits `statistics.total.percentage` (e.g., by grepping jscpd's own documentation or running `jscpd --reporters json` on a sample fixture) and add that verification reference to the Grounding section, since the cited backfill code only confirms `statistics.total.clones`.
evidence: scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py line 83: `return int(data["statistics"]["total"]["clones"])` — only clones, no percentage read
Plan Grounding: 'CONFIRMED grep -n "jscpd\\|statistics" ... reads statistics.total.clones from the JSON report'
AC1: 'run_jscpd in analyzers/_jscpd.py parses statistics.total.clones (int) + statistics.total.percentage (float)'
findings.finding: The Grounding section cites the existing _measure_clone_count to substantiate run_jscpd's field-parsing behavior, but the existing seam reads ONLY statistics.total.clones (line 83); it never reads statistics.total.percentage. AC1's claim that run_jscpd 'parses statistics.total.clones (int) + statistics.total.percentage (float)' is only half-grounded by the cited evidence — .percentage is a new field not present in or validated by the existing backfill seam.
findings.location: Grounding section (E4/G1G2) + AC1
scenarios: If jscpd's JSON output for the installed version does not include statistics.total.percentage (or places it at a different path), run_jscpd silently raises KeyError with no fallback — the grounding cites the existing code as evidence this field is readable, but the existing code never exercised it.
The test_parse_statistics_total AC1 test will need to cover a field the backfill never validated, so the grounding claim overstates what the cited code proves.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a grounding line such as: 'CONFIRMED statistics.total.percentage present in jscpd JSON schema via [reference to jscpd docs or sample output]' to distinguish the new field from the already-validated clones field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `statistics.total.percentage` exists in jscpd's actual JSON output schema (e.g., by running `npx jscpd --reporters json` against a fixture and inspecting the report, or citing the jscpd docs/changelog for the installed version), and document the exact key path in the plan.
evidence: scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py line 83: `return int(data["statistics"]["total"]["clones"])` — only clones, no percentage
Grounding claims the grep confirms both fields, but the grep pattern 'jscpd\|statistics' matches the file generically; the actual field `statistics.total.percentage` is absent from the codebase
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 65 shows jscpd invoked as `npx jscpd ... --reporters json` but no jscpd output schema is cited or verified anywhere in the repo
findings.finding: The plan asserts `run_jscpd` in `_jscpd.py` will parse `statistics.total.percentage` (float) from jscpd's JSON output, grounding this in the existing `_measure_clone_count` seam — but the actual backfill code reads ONLY `statistics.total.clones` (line 83); `statistics.total.percentage` is never read anywhere in the codebase. This is an unverified jscpd JSON-schema capability assertion.
findings.location: AC1: '`run_jscpd` in `analyzers/_jscpd.py` parses `statistics.total.clones` (int) + `statistics.total.percentage` (float)'; Grounding section: 'CONFIRMED grep ... reads `statistics.total.clones`'
scenarios: If jscpd's JSON output does not include `statistics.total.percentage` (e.g., it appears only under a different key or sub-object in the installed version), `run_jscpd` will raise a KeyError at runtime, causing the adapter to fail in unexpected ways.
jscpd's output schema has changed across major versions — without a version pin or schema verification, the percentage field may be named differently (e.g., `percentage` vs `percent`).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line jscpd invocation in the plan's Grounding section that captures and quotes the relevant `statistics.total` JSON object from actual jscpd output, confirming both `clones` and `percentage` keys exist. Alternatively, treat `percentage` as best-effort and guard with `.get('percentage')` with a fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact key name for the duplicated-lines percentage field in jscpd's JSON output (run `jscpd --reporters json --output /tmp/out .` on any small directory and inspect the `statistics.total` object), then update AC1 and the test fixture to use the confirmed key name.
evidence: scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py line 83: `return int(data["statistics"]["total"]["clones"])` — only `.clones` is read, `.percentage` is never accessed
No jscpd fixture file or sample JSON output exists anywhere in the repo to confirm the `statistics.total.percentage` key name
Evidence class: Unverified — the specific `statistics.total.percentage` key name cannot be confirmed from in-repo evidence alone
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 65 uses `npx jscpd ... --format python`, a different invocation that cannot cross-validate the JSON schema
findings.finding: AC1 mandates parsing `statistics.total.percentage` (float) from jscpd's JSON report, but no in-repo evidence confirms this exact key name exists. The existing backfill seam only reads `statistics.total.clones` (line 83); no fixture or sample jscpd JSON output is present in the repo. The field name is Unverified — if jscpd actually emits a differently-named key (e.g., `duplicatedPercentage`, `percent`, or nested differently), `run_jscpd` will raise a KeyError at runtime.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC1: `run_jscpd` parses `statistics.total.clones` (int) + `statistics.total.percentage` (float)
scenarios: An implementer writes `data['statistics']['total']['percentage']` following the plan; jscpd actually emits `data['statistics']['total']['duplicatedPercentage']` (or similar), causing a KeyError on every live invocation.
The integration test (test_parse_statistics_total) passes with a hand-rolled fixture using the assumed key name, shipping a silent runtime bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `jscpd --reporters json --output /tmp/jscpd-probe .` on a trivial temp directory, inspect `/tmp/jscpd-probe/jscpd-report.json`, confirm the exact key under `statistics.total` for the percentage value, and hard-code that confirmed name in AC1. Commit a minimal sample `jscpd-report.json` as a test fixture so future reviewers can audit the field name without needing jscpd installed.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the exact fields the backfill seam already reads' and then lists both `statistics.total.clones` AND `statistics.total.percentage`, implying the backfill seam already extracts the percentage field. In fact `_measure_clone_count` (backfill line 83) reads ONLY `statistics.total.clones`; `statistics.total.percentage` is not present anywhere in the backfill script. The shared helper therefore cannot be a pure extract of the existing seam — it must add new parsing logic for the percentage field.
findings.location: Decisions section: 'FIELDS: read statistics.total.clones (count) and statistics.total.percentage (duplicated-lines %) from the jscpd JSON report — the exact fields the backfill seam already reads.'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the exact fields the backfill seam already reads' to 'clones is read by the existing seam; percentage is new — the shared helper extends the seam to also parse statistics.total.percentage'.

findings.finding: The plan states the shared helper reads 'the exact fields the backfill seam already reads,' but the existing `_measure_clone_count` only reads `statistics.total.clones` — `statistics.total.percentage` is a NEW field not read by the backfill. This claim misstates the seam's scope, risks misleading the implementer about whether the field path is validated by prior usage, and leaves a hidden gap: the shared helper must now return a richer object (both fields), yet the plan gives no contract for how the backfill's current `int`-returning usage is reconciled with a multi-field return type while keeping backfill behavior 'unchanged'.
findings.location: Decisions section: 'FIELDS: read statistics.total.clones (count) and statistics.total.percentage … the exact fields the backfill seam already reads'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the FIELDS decision to: 'run_jscpd returns a dataclass/dict with `clones: int` and `percentage: float`; the backfill migration extracts `.clones` to preserve its current behavior. `statistics.total.percentage` is a new field — verify against a jscpd JSON sample in the PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `logger.warning` call in `run_jscpd` (or the adapter) when returning `Unavailable`, emitting the reason string so failures are visible in application logs.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of logging, metrics, or tracing in the plan
Unavailable carries a reason string, but nothing surfaces it to any log sink
findings.finding: The plan introduces no observability (logging) for the failure path: when `run_jscpd` returns `Unavailable`, operators have no way to discover why duplication data is absent without attaching a debugger.
findings.location: Approach & rationale; analyzers/_jscpd.py
scenarios: jscpd silently fails on CI; duplication metric shows N/A in dashboard; no log entry explains why; operator cannot distinguish 'jscpd not installed' from 'jscpd crashed' from 'network mount timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` to `_jscpd.py` and emit `logger.warning('jscpd unavailable: %s', reason)` before returning the `Unavailable` value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a `timeout` value for the `subprocess.run` call in `run_jscpd` (or document that it is intentionally unbounded and callers are responsible for timeouts), and handle `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` as an `Unavailable` result.
evidence: ABSENCE: no `timeout=` parameter mentioned for subprocess call
Existing backfill script grounding confirmed bare invocation with no timeout
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the `jscpd` subprocess invocation; on a large or pathological repo it could hang indefinitely, blocking the analysis pipeline.
findings.location: Approach & rationale; analyzers/_jscpd.py
scenarios: jscpd hangs on a symlink loop or very large monorepo; the analysis pipeline worker is blocked indefinitely; if the adapter is called in a request path, the request times out at a higher layer with no meaningful error
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `timeout=60` (or a configurable constant `_JSCPD_TIMEOUT_S = 60`) to `subprocess.run(...)` and catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` alongside other failure modes, mapping it to `Unavailable`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that bare `jscpd` subprocess invocation resolves correctly on Windows (where npm places `jscpd.cmd` on PATH), or document Windows as unsupported for this analyzer, to avoid silent Unavailable when the tool is actually installed.
evidence: Plan states: "Invocation stays BARE `jscpd` (npx explicitly out of scope) so the backfills behavior is unchanged."
Plan states: "the existing `_measure_clone_count` shells the BARE binary `[\"jscpd\", ...]`"
On Windows, `npm install -g jscpd` places `jscpd.cmd` on PATH; `subprocess(['jscpd', ...], shell=False)` raises FileNotFoundError even when the tool is installed
Windows is an explicitly supported platform in the rebar support matrix
findings.finding: On Windows, npm-installed CLI tools like jscpd are wrapped as `.cmd` batch scripts. Python's `subprocess` with `shell=False` (the default) does not resolve `.cmd` extensions when given a bare binary name `["jscpd", ...]`. This means a Windows user with jscpd correctly installed will still hit `FileNotFoundError`, which the plan routes to `Unavailable` — silently degrading instead of returning results. The tool is present but unreachable via the specified invocation.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: "Invocation stays BARE `jscpd`" and AC: `grep -q "\[.\?.jscpd" scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
scenarios: Windows developer workstation with jscpd installed via `npm install -g jscpd`: `analyze(root)` returns `Unavailable` instead of duplication metrics, because `jscpd` (without `.cmd`) is not found by subprocess; the feature silently produces no data on a supported platform where the dependency is satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `shutil.which('jscpd')` to resolve the full executable path (which handles `.cmd` on Windows) before invoking via subprocess, or pass `shell=True` on Windows. Alternatively, document that this analyzer requires a POSIX platform and gate with a `sys.platform` check that returns a clear Unavailable reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly justify the `_jscpd` private module abstraction against the ≥3 call-sites threshold — either name a third anticipated consumer (e.g., a future CI runner or report generator) or add a rationale statement explaining why the invocation-drift risk alone warrants a dedicated shared module with only 2 current call-sites.
evidence: "Extract the backfill jscpd runner + statistics.total read into analyzers/_jscpd.run_jscpd; both the backfill script and the new adapter call it."
Only two named callers: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py` and `analyzers/jscpd_dup.py`
No third call-site named or anticipated in the plan text
findings.finding: The new `_jscpd.run_jscpd` abstraction is justified by only 2 call-sites (backfill script + new adapter), falling below the ≥3 real call-sites threshold the G5 rubric requires before introducing a shared abstraction.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Files section — `analyzers/_jscpd.py` (new)
scenarios: If the shared runner is only ever called from two places, the abstraction adds indirection without meeting the YAGNI/Rule-of-Three threshold; the anti-drift motivation is real but could be achieved by a simpler inline dedup or a doc note rather than a dedicated private module.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section, e.g.: 'A dedicated `_jscpd` module is warranted despite only 2 current call-sites because jscpd invocation flags are brittle (path, format, output dir) and divergence between the backfill and the adapter would silently corrupt historical comparisons; this is the canonical anti-drift rationale for early extraction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in the Approach section citing the sibling `_scc.py` / `_jscpd.py` pattern (ticket 18f9) as the established convention that justifies a 2-call-site shared helper, so reviewers don't misread this as a premature abstraction.
evidence: Plan: 'Extract the backfill jscpd runner … into analyzers/_jscpd.run_jscpd; both the backfill script and the new adapter call it.'
scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py:68-83 — sole existing call-site (_measure_clone_count); adapter is the only additional one.

Ticket 18f9 (sibling) creates `analyzers/_scc.py` with the identical 2-call-site structure (engine_b_backends + scc_loc.py), establishing the pattern — but the current plan does not cite this as the precedent for accepting 2 call-sites.
findings.finding: `_jscpd.run_jscpd` has only 2 confirmed call-sites at ship time (the new `jscpd_dup.py` adapter + the re-pointed backfill script), below the ≥3 threshold before a shared-helper abstraction is justified by Rule-of-Three. The plan's invocation-drift rationale is sound but is not explicitly tied to the parallel `_scc.py` 2-call-site convention being set by sibling ticket 18f9 — without that acknowledgement the abstraction looks premature rather than deliberately pattern-consistent.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Files section
scenarios: A reviewer applying the Rule-of-Three strictly would flag this as premature abstraction; the plan gives no reference to the established `_scc.py` convention that makes 2 call-sites acceptable here.
If the backfill script is later retired (it is a one-time backfill), `run_jscpd` drops to a single call-site with no migration path specified.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The `_jscpd.py` module follows the same `_<tool>.py` private-helper convention established by the sibling scc adapter (ticket 18f9 / `analyzers/_scc.py`), where a 2-call-site shared runner is the intended shape for each deterministic CLI adapter in this package.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` to the Files section so the new package is importable.
evidence: The plan lists two new files inside `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` but does not list `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py`.
Existing parallel packages in `src/rebar/metrics/` (e.g. `adapters/`) all have an `__init__.py` — confirmed `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` exists.
Without `__init__.py`, `from rebar.metrics.analyzers import _jscpd` (required by both the adapter and the backfill script) will fail with ModuleNotFoundError at import time.
Dep 3b30's file_impact does not create this `__init__.py` either (3b30 targets `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`, a flat module, not a package).
findings.finding: The `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` package `__init__.py` is not listed in the Files section but is required to make the new `analyzers/` directory importable as a Python package.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/jscpd_dup.py (new), src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_jscpd.py (new)`
scenarios: Implementation starts from the plan's Files list and omits __init__.py, causing all import-based tests to fail.
AC test `test_single_runner_reused` (which asserts both modules reference `_jscpd.run_jscpd`) will fail with ImportError before any assertion runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py (new, empty)` to the Files list alongside `jscpd_dup.py` and `_jscpd.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py` to the parent epic (839b) file_impact and justify the scope extension there, or extract the backfill re-delegation into a separate story, so this leaf's deliverables are a strict subset of the parent's declared scope.
evidence: Epic 839b file_impact lists: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py, src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/, src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py, pyproject.toml, src/rebar/_config_schema.py, docs/config.md — no backfill script
Leaf plan: 'scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py (delegate)' and AC5: grep check on backfill script
Leaf rationale: 'one runner = no invocation drift' — a design benefit not articulated in the parent plan
findings.finding: The leaf plans to modify scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py (re-delegating its jscpd invocation to the shared _jscpd.run_jscpd helper), but this file is absent from the parent epic's (839b) file_impact list and is not mentioned in any parent acceptance criterion. The leaf delivers a backfill-script refactor that the parent's plan does not contain.
findings.location: Files section: 'scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py (delegate)'; Approach & rationale: 'Extract the backfill jscpd runner… both the backfill script and the new adapter call it'
scenarios: If the backfill script refactor introduces a regression, it was never sanctioned by the parent plan, so the scope of this ticket was silently broader than agreed.
A parallel ticket (e.g. a future backfill migration story) may also plan to touch that script, causing a merge conflict or duplicated effort.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py — re-delegate jscpd invocation to shared _jscpd.run_jscpd' to epic 839b's file_impact (which stales its plan-review and forces re-review), then the leaf's scope becomes a valid subset.

findings.finding: The Decisions section claims `statistics.total.percentage` is one of 'the exact fields the backfill seam already reads', but the actual backfill code (`_measure_clone_count`, line 83) reads only `statistics.total.clones`. `statistics.total.percentage` is a net-new field not verified by any existing codebase code, making this an unverified capability assertion about jscpd's JSON output schema.
findings.location: Decisions section: 'FIELDS: read statistics.total.clones (count) and statistics.total.percentage (duplicated-lines %) from the jscpd JSON report — the exact fields the backfill seam already reads.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note to the Decisions section: 'NOTE: `statistics.total.percentage` is NOT currently read by the backfill seam; confirm the exact key name from jscpd docs or a sample report before implementing.' Alternatively, add a test fixture containing a real jscpd JSON output snippet asserting both keys are present.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `statistics.total.percentage` is 'the exact field the backfill seam already reads', but the actual backfill script only reads `statistics.total.clones` — `.percentage` is never accessed anywhere in the codebase. Its exact field name in jscpd's JSON output is therefore Unverified, making it an asserted-but-unverified external capability the adapter depends on.
findings.location: Decisions section: 'FIELDS: read `statistics.total.clones` (count) and `statistics.total.percentage` (duplicated-lines %) from the jscpd JSON report — the exact fields the backfill seam already reads.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line empirical verification step: run `jscpd --reporters json --output /tmp/jscpd_out <fixture>` and `cat /tmp/jscpd_out/jscpd-report.json | python -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d['statistics']['total'].keys())"` to confirm the exact key. Then update `_JSCPD.py` to use the verified key name and add it as a confirmed field in the unit test fixture.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
